name: Hungarian Seven-Layer Cake
recipeIngredient: (for a sweeter icing, use semisweet for al
recipeInstructions:
If you have only two or three 8-inch pans, and if they will fit on the same rack, adjust the rack to the lowest position in the oven. If you have more pans than will fit on one rack, adjust two racks; one to the lowest position and the other closer to the middle. Preheat oven to 350°F.
Cut seven circles of wax paper to fit the 8-inch pans. Butter as many pans as you have, line them with the papers and butter the papers. Set the prepared pans aside and reserve the extra circles of wax paper.
In a small bowl of an electric mixer, beat the egg yolks and about half of the sugar at high speed for about 5 minutes until very pale and thick. Gradually add the flour on low speed and beat, scraping the bowl with a rubber spatula and beating only until the flour is incorporated. The mixture will be very thick. Remove it from the mixer using your index finger to scrape the beaters clean.
Add salt to the egg whites in another large bowl of an electric mixer. Beat until the whites hold a soft shape. Reduce speed to moderate, gradually add the remaining sugar, then increase speed and beat until the whites hold a firm shape.
Add about 1/2 cup of the beaten whites to the yolks and stir it in. Then stir in another 1/2 cup. Gradually fold in all but about 2 cups of the whites. Fold the yolks into the remaining whites.
Spread about 6 1/2 cups of batter evenly across the prepared pans, touching the sides completely around each pan; do not measure precisely, just approximate. Bake for 15 minutes.
If using more than one rack, reverse the pans top to bottom once during baking so that each layer bakes well on the lowest rack. When done, tops will be barely colored, and edges may show signs of coming away from the pans.
Lay out a large, smooth cotton or linen towel. When layers are finished, invert them onto the towel, remove pans, and peel off papers. If the bottom is baked dry enough, papers should peel off in one piece; if not, tear off pieces at a time. Turn layers right side up immediately to avoid sticking.
Let cool completely before proceeding.
Prepare remaining batter for another round of baking using the same method.
For the filling and icing:
Place chocolate in a small double boiler over hot water on moderate heat until partially melted. Remove from heat and stir until fully melted.
In a saucepan, mix water with sugar and instant coffee; place over moderate heat and stir until sugar dissolves completely and mixture boils.
Meanwhile, beat egg yolks at high speed in a bowl until pale lemon-colored.
When syrup is ready, lower mixer speed and slowly incorporate syrup into yolks. Add warm chocolate and beat until smooth.
Beat in butter piece by piece until well blended.
Spread a thin layer of buttercream over cake layers using a long, narrow spatula, spreading to edges smoothly. Spread evenly without making too thick; place all but the top layer right side up.
Place top layer upside down before covering sides with filling.
If icing is not smooth, process in food processor briefly for perfect finish.
Spread icing evenly over cake and cover sides.
Remove wax paper strips carefully by pulling toward narrow ends.
Refrigerate several hours to set icing. Serve at room temperature or chilled; cut with a sharp knife. If frozen, chill until firm before wrapping tightly and thaw overnight in refrigerator.
Top can be garnished with chocolate shavings and coated lightly with cocoa powder for decoration. (Recipe from Maida Heatter's Book of Great Chocolate Desserts). This cake can be stored refrigerated for up to two days or frozen for longer storage.)
